title: TabReD: Analyzing Pitfalls and Filling the Gaps in Tabular Deep Learning Benchmarks
authors: Ivan Rubachev, Nikolay Kartashev, Yury Gorishniy, Artem Babenko (Yandex; HSE University)
venue: arXiv:2406.19380 (preprint, submitted Jun 2024, v-final Oct 2024); subsequently ICLR 2025
tokenization_method: None of its own. TabReD is a BENCHMARK, not a tokenizer. Its relevance to this topic is that it is the only public tabular benchmark whose datasets simultaneously have (a) MANY features (median 261, up to 1026) from real industrial feature-engineering pipelines and (b) time-based train/val/test splits reflecting gradual temporal drift. So it is the closest existing fair-evaluation harness for the exact regime this topic targets: 70+ heterogeneous, partly time-varying features. It re-runs a battery of tokenization/embedding regimes head-to-head on this regime: per-feature attention tokenizers (FT-Transformer), MLP + numerical-feature embeddings (MLP-PLR, the periodic-linear-ReLU embeddings of Gorishniy et al. 2022), cross-network feature interaction (DCNv2), prompt/column tokens (Trompt), retrieval-augmented DL (TabR, ModernNCA), plus GBDTs (XGBoost/LightGBM/CatBoost) and plain MLP/ResNet/SNN baselines. The headline: under time-based splits on feature-rich data, GBDT and MLP-with-numerical-embeddings win; most fancier tokenizers/retrieval do NOT transfer.
feature_types_handled: Mixed numerical + categorical, real-world industrial. Predominantly NUMERICAL-heavy (production feature-engineering pipelines emit mostly continuous engineered features); categorical fields exist but the paper does not isolate them. Datasets carry gradual TEMPORAL drift (data collected over time) but are modeled as flat rows ordered by timestamp — i.e. time is the SPLIT axis, not a per-row sequence. Missing values present and handled. Crucially: NO explicit time-series/sequence structure per row; this is the static-snapshot-with-drift setting, not the temporal-patch setting.
token_granularity: Delegated to each evaluated model. FT-Transformer uses one token per feature (per-feature tokens); MLP-PLR uses per-numerical-feature embedding vectors concatenated; GBDTs use no tokens (split-based). TabReD itself imposes no granularity — it is the arena where per-feature vs no-token regimes compete on wide tables.
numerical_strategy: Benchmark-level preprocessing follows Gorishniy et al. (2021;2024): quantile normalization of numerical features (NaNs replaced by the variable mean, i.e. zero after quantile-normalization). On top of that, the evaluated MLP-PLR applies the periodic + linear + ReLU numerical-feature embeddings of Gorishniy et al. 2022 — and this is precisely the technique TabReD finds DOES transfer to the wide/temporal setting.
categorical_strategy: Benchmark-level: standard learned/encoded categoricals following Gorishniy et al.; unseen categorical values in val/test are mapped to a special 'unknown' category. The paper does NOT propose or analyze a categorical encoder and does NOT isolate categorical-feature performance, so it offers no verdict on categorical tokenization specifically.
high_cardinality_handling: Not isolated or analyzed. The industrial datasets surely contain some high-cardinality fields (e.g. product/road-graph identifiers in Maps Routing) but the paper treats the feature set as a whole and reports no high-cardinality-specific axis. Treat high-cardinality conclusions as UNVERIFIED from this work.
temporal_handling: Time is used as the EVALUATION-SPLIT axis (time-based train/val/test via a sliding window), NOT as a per-row sequence to tokenize. Every TabReD dataset has timestamps and gradual temporal drift. The paper's central methodological claim is that time-based splits change model ranking vs random splits — but it does NOT build temporal patches, channels, or recurrence. So it informs the temporal-static-fusion layer about EVALUATION discipline and drift robustness, not about how to tokenize time-varying signals.
scales_to_many_features: This is the single most relevant property for this topic. TabReD datasets have median 261 features (Maps Routing 1026, HomeCredit Default 696, Sberbank Housing 387, Homesite Insurance 296, Delivery ETA 225, Cooking Time 195, Ecom Offers 119, Weather 98) vs <60 features for most academic datasets analyzed (out of 100 surveyed). Sample sizes 20K-7M (4 datasets >1M full size). The paper explicitly flags that FT-Transformer's self-attention is QUADRATIC in the number of features, making it slower on TabReD's wide tables, and that this cost matters here. So TabReD is direct evidence on the >70-feature axis: per-feature attention tokenizers pay a real cost at this width and only rank as runner-up, while MLP + per-numerical-feature embeddings stay efficient and best.
downstream_model: MLP / ResNet / SNN, FT-Transformer, DCNv2, Trompt, MLP-PLR (MLP + numerical embeddings), TabR / ModernNCA (retrieval-augmented DL), plus GBDTs (XGBoost, LightGBM, CatBoost), RandomForest, Linear. Tuned with Optuna, AdamW, 15 seeds, statistical significance via std-dev rule + Tamhane's T2 test.
pretraining_objective: n/a for the benchmark. It additionally evaluates two augmentation/self-supervised training recipes ('MLP aug.' long-training-with-augmentations and 'MLP aug. rec.' reconstruction-loss, from Rubachev et al. 2022) and finds they mostly do NOT transfer to TabReD (negative relative change vs MLP on average).
key_results: Average rank over 8 datasets (Table 3; lower=better): MLP-PLR ens. 2.4, XGBoost 2.9, LightGBM 3.1, CatBoost 3.4, MLP-PLR 3.8, MLP ens. 4.1, FT-Transformer 4.8, MLP 5.0, Trompt 5.4, ModernNCA 5.6, ResNet 5.8, MLP aug. 6.0, TabR 6.0, SNN 6.6, MLP aug. rec. 6.8, DCNv2 7.6, RandomForest 7.8, Linear 8.8. Takeaways: (1) GBDT and MLP-with-numerical-embeddings (MLP-PLR) are the overall best; numerical embeddings transfer. (2) Ensembling transfers (MLP-PLR ens. is #1). (3) FT-Transformer is a runner-up but slower (quadratic-in-features attention). (4) SNN/DCNv2/ResNet/Trompt are no better than plain MLP. (5) Retrieval models (TabR, ModernNCA) UNDERPERFORM on TabReD (on the prior benchmark of Gorishniy et al. 2024 TabR was +1.34% over MLP; on TabReD it is -2.78%). (6) Improved training recipes also fail to transfer (MLP aug. -1.48%, MLP aug. rec. -1.98% vs MLP). Section 5.4: switching from random to time-based splits changes absolute scores, variances, AND model ranking — XGBoost's margin over MLPs shrinks under time-based splits (it may exploit time-leakage on random splits), and TabR-S sometimes beats MLP/XGBoost on time-based splits.
baselines_compared: GBDTs (XGBoost/LightGBM/CatBoost), RandomForest, Linear, MLP, ResNet, SNN, DCNv2, FT-Transformer, Trompt, TabR, ModernNCA, MLP-PLR, plus ensembles and augmentation recipes. Also a meta-analysis of 100 existing classification/regression datasets across prior benchmarks (Table 1) for feature counts, sample sizes, leakage, synthetic/non-tabular issues, and timestamp availability.
transferable_idea: Two reusable things for a 70+-heterogeneous-feature tokenizer project. (1) EVIDENCE: per-numerical-feature embeddings (the PLR / periodic-linear-ReLU embeddings of Gorishniy et al. 2022) are the one learned-tokenization idea that demonstrably KEEPS its benefit on wide (median-261-feature) industrial tables with temporal drift — they make a plain MLP competitive with GBDTs and rank #1 when ensembled. So if you must pick one tokenization upgrade for this topic's setting, per-numerical-feature embeddings + ensembling are the proven default. (2) METHODOLOGY: any new tokenizer claim for this topic MUST be validated under time-based (out-of-time) splits, not random splits — random splits give optimistic, sometimes leakage-inflated rankings, and the model ordering genuinely flips. TabReD's 8 datasets are a ready, public, feature-rich, time-split testbed.
limitations: Author-stated: TabReD is biased toward industry ML (large samples, heavy numerical feature engineering, temporal drift); does NOT cover medicine, science, social-science domains; lacks precise feature names/descriptions (so it can't be used for LLM-on-feature-name approaches like TabLLM); is not a full replacement for academic benchmarks. Only 8 datasets. Features are mostly engineered numerical, so it under-tests categorical / high-cardinality / true time-series tokenization. The retrieval-failure and training-recipe-failure explanations are explicitly HYPOTHESES (multicollinearity/noisy features + temporal-shift violating the retrieval assumption), not proven causal claims.
pitfalls: 1) This is a benchmark, NOT a tokenizer — cite it as evidence/evaluation context, never as a technique. 2) The 'retrieval models fail because of temporal shift / noisy features' explanation is the authors' HYPOTHESIS ('we hypothesize', 'might'), not demonstrated — do not propagate it as established mechanism. 3) Features are mostly engineered NUMERICAL; TabReD says little about categorical or high-cardinality tokenization, so do not read its 'MLP-PLR wins' result as a verdict on categorical encoders. 4) 'Time is the split axis, not per-row sequence' — TabReD validates drift-aware EVALUATION, not how to tokenize time-varying features; it does not address the temporal-patch part of this topic's temporal-static-fusion layer. 5) XGBoost looking strong on random splits may be exploiting time-leakage — a warning that any tokenizer tuned/validated on random splits of a temporally-ordered dataset may report inflated gains. 6) Only 8 datasets and a single research group's preprocessing; generalization of the ranking beyond industrial-numerical-heavy data is unproven.
relevance_to_topic: Directly central: it is the only public benchmark whose feature counts (median 261, up to 1026) and temporal-drift structure match this topic's 70+-mixed, partly-time-varying setting, and it tells you which tokenization regimes survive there — per-numerical-feature embeddings + ensembling do, per-feature attention pays a quadratic cost and is only runner-up, retrieval and fancy training recipes do not transfer.
code_available: yes — https://github.com/yandex-research/tabred (datasets, preprocessing, training/tuning code). Appears maintained and aligned with the paper; some industrial datasets released as preprocessed/anonymized.
